result:
I keep thinking about the way light lands on the inside of my forearm in the late afternoon—how it makes the bluish tributaries under the skin look like tiny, illuminated road maps.  
If you followed them, you’d travel from the bend of my elbow down to the wrist’s delta, and somewhere along that short journey you’d cross the scar I got at seven, when I tried to open a coconut with a steak knife because I wanted to see what the milk tasted like straight from the shell.  
I remember the shock of seeing my own blood more than the pain; it felt like betrayal—like my body had kept a secret from me and then shouted it.  

That’s probably the first time I understood that anything can break open, and what spills out isn’t always what you expect.  
I’ve been practicing that lesson ever since: cracking open hours, conversations, even people, just to see what luminous stuff might leak.  
Mostly it’s ordinary: coffee that’s gone tepid, a joke that lands wrong, the small click of the front door at 2 a.m. when my kid sneaks back from whatever new country teenagers invent after dark.  
But sometimes it’s starlight—once, a stranger on the train told me he still keeps his grandfather’s broken pocket watch because “broken is heavier, so time can’t run away as fast.”  
He said it flatly, like he was reading the weather, and then stared out the window until we reached Union Station, where he vanished into the crowd carrying nothing.  

I think we’re all walking around carrying pocket watches that don’t tick, pretending we’re on time.  
Meanwhile the planet spins at 1,674 kilometers an hour, which means by the time you finish reading this sentence you’ll have traveled about thirty kilometers through space without feeling a thing.  
I find that comforting and unsettling in equal measure—like being kissed on the back of the neck by someone who refuses to leave a note.  

I used to want certainty the way other people want wealth: a locked safe full of irrefutable answers.  
Now I want the hush right before the answer, the breath held between question and reply, the white space where anything can happen.  
If you stand there long enough, you can hear the arteries humming their little road songs, promising that every scar is just a shortcut to the next astonishment.
